design_philosophy.core_loop: Select fighter → compete in tournament bracket across global stages → defeat opponent's health bar through combo execution and plane-switching mechanics → advance to next opponent or boss
design_philosophy.primary_tension: One-on-one fighting skill execution with environmental stage hazards and the two-plane battle system creating spatial complexity
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Compete as an international martial arts champion in a global King of Fighters tournament, mastering diverse fighting styles and proving dominance
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: The two-plane battle system is philosophically central—fighters can move between foreground and background planes, with hazards in one plane forcing tactical plane management. This distinguishes Fatal Fury 2 from pure 2D fighters and creates a unique spatial depth puzzle layered atop fighting mechanics. The game emphasizes international character diversity (Korean taekwondo, Japanese ninjutsu, Chinese tai chi, Australian wrestling) as cultural representation rather than palette swaps.

difficulty.lives_system: Single match loss progression; player faces all eight playable characters sequentially, then four boss characters. Matches are discrete; losing a match likely resets progress
difficulty.checkpointing: Bracket-based progression with matches as discrete units. Bonus rounds occur after every fourth match for point accumulation
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Character selection provides some difficulty variance—players choose from eight fighters with distinct movesets and fighting styles, allowing strategic selection for different opponent matchups

progression.unlock_mechanism: Boss characters (Billy Kane, Axel Hawk, Laurence Blood, Wolfgang Krauser) unlock after defeating all eight playable fighters. Character-specific clone battles included in progression
replayability_hooks: Eight selectable playable characters with distinct fighting styles and movesets
Four non-playable boss encounters requiring specific strategies
Two-player multiplayer mode extends beyond single-player campaign
Global tournament stages with environmental hazards affecting plane-switching tactics
Bonus rounds between match clusters reward point accumulation

world_building_techniques: Tournament structure framing device: mysterious nobleman sponsors new King of Fighters tournament following Geese Howard's death
Character roster represents international martial arts schools: South Korea (taekwondo), Japan (ninjutsu, judo), Hong Kong (tai chi), Australia (wrestling), UK (bojutsu)
Boss progression reveals narrative: mysterious sponsor systematically defeats original tournament veterans, searching for Geese's killer
Character intros and victory poses provide minimal personality/backstory hints
Stage locations span global venues (Australia explicitly mentioned), supporting tournament-across-continents narrative

boss_design.philosophy: Bosses represent escalating threat and narrative payoff. They are non-playable characters distinct from the eight tournament fighters, with stronger AI and exclusive movesets. Boss encounters cap the campaign progression after all playable fighters are defeated.
notable_bosses: Billy Kane - bojutsu master seeking revenge for Geese's death; thematic connection to protagonist victory
Axel Hawk - retired heavyweight boxer attempting comeback; endurance-based fighting style
Laurence Blood - former matador using bullfighting methods (censored to 'B.' in Western versions due to 'Bloody Spin' voice removal); stylistic uniqueness
Wolfgang Krauser - German nobleman as final boss; explicitly seeking to defeat Geese's killer, tying to narrative motivation

faction_indicators: International martial arts schools (Korean taekwondo vs. Japanese ninjutsu vs. Chinese tai chi) create implicit cultural affiliations
Kim Kaphwan fights 'in the name of justice'—explicit moral positioning distinct from other competitors
Cheng Sinzan explicitly greedy and wealth-focused (seeking to open training hall)
Andy Bogard and Mai Shiranui paired as ninjutsu practitioners with romantic subtext
danger_signals: Billy Kane explicitly vengeful—pursuing Bogard brothers and Joe for Geese's death
Laurence Blood's bullfighting heritage and violent past (matador background) suggests brutality
Wolfgang Krauser as German nobleman with imperial/authoritarian aesthetics
Boss encounters positioned as narrative escalation of threat level
environmental_storytelling: Global tournament stages (Australia prominently featured) establish international competition scope
Stage hazards (electrified wires, bull stampedes) reflect regional environments and cultural contexts
Two-plane battle system creates stage-specific tactical challenges (some planes have hazards, forcing strategic plane-switching)

sources_used: tcrf - The Cutting Room Floor (Fatal Fury 2 SNES page): Regional differences, censorship details, development context
wikipedia - Fatal Fury 2 entry: Gameplay mechanics, character roster with backstories, boss descriptions, plot summary, release information
